revision.comment: 願い事をする寺社の総称。たたき台につき、加筆乞ふ
revision.text:
'''祈願所'''（きがんじょ）は[[神社]]、[[寺院]]の1つで、特に人々が[[神]][[仏]]に願い事を行う寺社を指す。寺院の場合は'''[[祈願寺]]'''、'''御願寺'''と言う。
また、[[天皇]]によって祈願寺となった寺院を、特に[[勅願寺]]と言う。
== 起源と歴史 == === 神道の場合 === 神社に祈願する事は、古くから行われている。特に[[平安時代]]後期には、[[宇佐八幡宮]]が[[源氏]]の[[氏神]]となった事により、『[[吾妻鏡]]』や『[[太平記]]』に見られるように、多くの[[武士]]によって神に祈願が為された。
=== 仏教の場合 === [[飛鳥時代]]に日本に伝来した[[仏教]]は、その後、国家の統制下に置かれ、寺院も国家によって建立されるようになった。その経緯から寺院では国家の発展、疫病防除、万民の健康を祈念するようになった。
平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。
[[鎌倉時代]]になると、仏教信仰が武士にも広まり、各武士の[[所領]]に当主や一族の繁栄や無事を祈る祈願寺が建立され、やがて戦死した一族を祀る[[菩提寺]]と一体化した。
[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、旅行が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社にされるようになった。
因みに、近代以前の祈願とは、例えば「～の願い事を叶えて頂いた暁には、燈籠を奉納します」のように神仏との契約であり、現代のように、願い事を行うだけではなかった。これらの契約によって行われた施設の補修や調度品の奉納などを、2005年現在も見る事が出来る。 <!--=== 祈願寺・勅願寺の認定 ===-->
== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]]
{{stub}} [[Category:日本の寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]]

'''祈願所'''（きがんじょ）は[[神社]]、[[寺院]]の1つで、特に人々が[[神]][[仏]]に願い事を行う寺社を指す。寺院の場合は'''祈願寺'''、'''御願寺'''と言う。
また、[[天皇]]によって祈願寺となった寺院を、特に'''[[勅願寺]]'''と言う。
== 起源と歴史 == === 神道の場合 === 神社に祈願する事は、古くから行われている。特に[[平安時代]]後期には、[[宇佐八幡宮]]が[[源氏]]の[[氏神]]となった事により、『[[吾妻鏡]]』や『[[太平記]]』に見られるように、多くの[[武士]]によって神に祈願が為された。
=== 仏教の場合 === [[飛鳥時代]]に日本に伝来した[[仏教]]は、その後、国家の統制下に置かれ、寺院も国家によって建立されるようになった。その経緯から寺院では国家の発展、疫病防除、万民の健康を祈念するようになった。
平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。
[[鎌倉時代]]になると、仏教信仰が武士にも広まり、各武士の[[所領]]に当主や一族の繁栄や無事を祈る祈願寺が建立され、やがて戦死した一族を祀る[[菩提寺]]と一体化した。
[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、旅行が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社にされるようになった。
因みに、近代以前の祈願とは、例えば「～の願い事を叶えて頂いた暁には、燈籠を奉納します」のように神仏との契約であり、現代のように、願い事を行うだけではなかった。これらの契約によって行われた施設の補修や調度品の奉納などを、2005年現在も見る事が出来る。 <!--=== 祈願寺・勅願寺の認定 ===-->
== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]]
{{stub}} [[Category:日本の寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]]

平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。

[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、[[旅行]]が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社になされるようになった。

== 起源と歴史 == === 神道の場合 === 神社に祈願する事は、古くから行われている。特に[[平安時代]]後期には、[[宇佐八幡宮]]が[[源氏]]の[[氏神]]となった事により、『[[吾妻鏡]]』や『[[太平記]]』に見られるように、多くの[[武士]]によって神に祈願が為された。
=== 仏教の場合 === [[飛鳥時代]]に日本に伝来した[[仏教]]は、その後、国家の統制下に置かれ、寺院も国家や有力[[氏族]]によって建立されるようになった。その経緯から寺院では国家の発展、疫病防除、万民の健康を祈念するようになった。
平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。
[[鎌倉時代]]になると、仏教信仰が武士にも広まり、各武士の[[所領]]に当主や一族の繁栄や無事を祈る祈願寺が建立され、やがて戦死した一族を祀る[[菩提寺]]と一体化した。
[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、[[旅行]]が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社になされるようになった。
因みに、近代以前の祈願とは、例えば「～の願い事を叶えて頂いた暁には、燈籠を奉納します」のように神仏との契約であり、現代のように、願い事を行うだけの一方的な関係ではなかった。これらの契約によって行われた施設の補修や調度品の奉納などを、2006年現在も見る事が出来る。 <!--=== 祈願寺・勅願寺の認定 ===-->
== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]]
{{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}} [[Category:寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きかんしよ}}

'''祈願所'''（きがんじょ）は[[神社]]、[[寺院]]の1つで、特に人々が[[神]][[仏]]に願い事を行う寺社を指す。寺院の場合は'''祈願寺'''、'''御願寺'''と言う。
また、[[天皇]]によって祈願寺となった寺院を、特に'''[[勅願寺]]'''と言う。
== 起源と歴史 == === 神道の場合 === 神社に祈願する事は、古くから行われている。特に[[平安時代]]後期には、[[宇佐八幡宮]]が[[源氏]]の[[氏神]]となった事により、『[[吾妻鏡]]』や『[[太平記]]』に見られるように、多くの[[武士]]によって神に祈願が為された。
=== 仏教の場合 === [[飛鳥時代]]に日本に伝来した[[仏教]]は、その後、国家の統制下に置かれ、寺院も国家や有力[[氏族]]によって建立されるようになった。その経緯から寺院では国家の発展、疫病防除、万民の健康を祈念するようになった。
平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。
[[鎌倉時代]]になると、仏教信仰が武士にも広まり、各武士の[[所領]]に当主や一族の繁栄や無事を祈る祈願寺が建立され、やがて戦死した一族を祀る[[菩提寺]]と一体化した。
[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、[[旅行]]が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社になされるようになった。
因みに、近代以前の祈願とは、例えば「～の願い事を叶えて頂いた暁には、燈籠を奉納します」のように神仏との契約であり、現代のように、願い事を行うだけの一方的な関係ではなかった。これらの契約によって行われた施設の補修や調度品の奉納などを、2006年現在も見る事が出来る。 <!--=== 祈願寺・勅願寺の認定 ===-->
== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]] {{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}} [[Category:寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きかんしよ}}

revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}
[[Category:Temples in Japan]] [[Category:Shrines in Japan]]

[[ja:祈願所]]

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== Related Topics ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺|1=chokugan-ji}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

[[ja:祈願所]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kigan-sho]][[Category:11]]

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006. == See Also! == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神|12=draft}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|12=draft}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺|12=draft}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記|12=draft}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族|12=draft}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.

Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=draft}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=draft}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Kami|ja|神|12=draft}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|12=draft}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺|12=draft}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記|12=draft}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族|12=draft}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=draft}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺|12=draft}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=draft}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=draft}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

revision.comment: 11 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:祈願所 up to rev 73487042
revision.text:
'''祈願所'''（きがんじょ）は[[神社]]、[[寺院]]の1つで、特に人々が[[神]][[仏]]に願い事を行う寺社を指す。寺院の場合は'''祈願寺'''、'''御願寺'''と言う。
また、[[天皇]]によって祈願寺となった寺院を、特に'''[[勅願寺]]'''と言う。
== 起源と歴史 == === 神道の場合 === 神社に祈願する事は、古くから行われている。特に[[平安時代]]後期には、[[宇佐八幡宮]]が[[源氏]]の[[氏神]]となった事により、『[[吾妻鏡]]』や『[[太平記]]』に見られるように、多くの[[武士]]によって神に祈願が為された。
=== 仏教の場合 === [[飛鳥時代]]に日本に伝来した[[仏教]]は、その後、国家の統制下に置かれ、寺院も国家や有力[[氏族]]によって建立されるようになった。その経緯から寺院では国家の発展、疫病防除、万民の健康を祈念するようになった。
平安時代になると、仏教信仰が[[貴族]]の間にも広まり、一部では[[奈良時代]]から既に行われてきたが、各氏族で個々に寺院を建立するようになり、そこで氏族の繁栄を祈念した。
[[鎌倉時代]]になると、仏教信仰が武士にも広まり、各武士の[[所領]]に当主や一族の繁栄や無事を祈る祈願寺が建立され、やがて戦死した一族を祀る[[菩提寺]]と一体化した。
[[室町時代]]になると、仏教信仰が庶民の間にも広まり、各地の寺院に願い事を行うのが一般的となり、願い事を書いた祈願文が奉納されるようになった。
[[江戸時代]]になると、[[旅行]]が大衆化し、それまでは近隣の寺社にのみ参詣していたが、[[善光寺]]や[[四国八十八箇所]]のように、遠隔地の寺社へも参詣するようになり、様々な祈願が各地の寺社になされるようになった。
因みに、近代以前の祈願とは、例えば「～の願い事を叶えて頂いた暁には、燈籠を奉納します」のように神仏との契約であり、現代のように、願い事を行うだけの一方的な関係ではなかった。これらの契約によって行われた施設の補修や調度品の奉納などを、2006年現在も見る事が出来る。 <!--=== 祈願寺・勅願寺の認定 ===-->
== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]] {{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}} [[Category:寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きかんしよ}}

== 関連項目 == * [[内道場]] - [[中国]][[皇帝]]の私的寺院 * [[プライベート・チャペル]] - [[ヨーロッパ]]の[[貴族]]の邸宅内にある個人用の[[礼拝堂]] {{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}} [[Category:寺|きかんしよ]] [[Category:神社|きかんしよ]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きかんしよ}}

In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺|12=draft}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|ja|太平記|12=draft}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|aristocracy|ja|貴族|12=draft}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=draft}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺|12=draft}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=draft}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=draft}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Reli-stub}}

}}

revision.text:
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] or [[:en:Buddhist temple|Buddhist temple]], particularly those where people offer prayers to [[:en:Deity|Kami]] or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddha|qq=}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Imperial prayer temples|ja|勅願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial prayer temples|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} became the tutelary shrine of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]]. Consequently, as depicted in texts like [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] and [[:en:Taiheiki|Taiheiki]], many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], which arrived in Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the [[:en:Nobility|aristocracy]]. While some practices began in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|qq=}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|qq=}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|qq=}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the [[:en:Nobility|aristocracy]]. While some practices began in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|qq=}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.

During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} became the tutelary shrine of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]]. Consequently, as depicted in texts like [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] and [[:en:Taiheiki|Taiheiki]], many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], which arrived in Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], was placed under state control. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11588473]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11588473]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] or [[:en:Buddhist temple|Buddhist temple]], particularly those where people offer prayers to [[:en:Deity|Kami]] or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q715632}} became the tutelary shrine of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]]. Consequently, as depicted in texts like [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] and [[:en:Taiheiki|Taiheiki]], many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], which arrived in Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the [[:en:Nobility|aristocracy]]. While some practices began in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|qq=|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
{{神道 横}}
