Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q715632}} became the tutelary shrine of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]]. Consequently, as depicted in texts like [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] and [[:en:Taiheiki|Taiheiki]], many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], which arrived in Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the [[:en:Nobility|aristocracy]]. While some practices began in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|qq=|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.

By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Consequently, as depicted in texts like [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] and [[:en:Taiheiki|Taiheiki]], many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], which arrived in Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the [[:en:Nobility|aristocracy]]. While some practices began in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q715632}} became the tutelary shrine of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]]. These temples often became intertwined with [[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]], which served as family memorial temples.
During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.

In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588473]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588473]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Buddhist temple}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Kami}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q715632}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|en|Taiheiki|WD=Q1152189|lt=Taiheiki}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocracy}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|WD=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocracy}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|WD=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.

Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=kiganjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祈願所]]
'''Kigansho''' (祈願所) refers to a specific type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} or {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|qid=Q5393308|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, particularly those where people offer prayers to {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=Kami|1=Deity}} or {{ill|Buddha|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha (title)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7055}} for their wishes to be granted. In the case of temples, they are called '''Kiganji''' (祈願寺) or '''Goganji''' (御願寺).
Additionally, temples designated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} for specific prayers are referred to as '''{{ill|Chokugan-ji|ja|勅願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugan-ji|qq=}}'''.
== Origins and History == === In Shinto === Prayers at Shinto shrines have been practiced since ancient times. During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q715632}} became the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|qid=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}}. Consequently, as depicted in texts like {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} and {{ill|Taiheiki|en|Taiheiki|qid=Q1152189|lt=Taiheiki|1=Taiheiki}}, many {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}} offered prayers to deities for their wishes.
=== In Buddhism === {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, which arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, was placed under state control. Temples were established by the government and powerful clans, and they served as centers for praying for national development, disease prevention, and the well-being of the people.
By the Heian period, Buddhist faith had spread among the {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|qid=Q134737|lt=aristocracy|1=Nobility}}. While some practices began in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, individual clans started constructing their own temples to pray for their prosperity.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Buddhism gained popularity among samurai. Lords and clans established {{ill|prayer temples|ja|祈願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayer temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588473}} within their domains to pray for their safety and prosperity. These temples often became intertwined with {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|qid=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji|1=Bodaiji}}, which served as family memorial temples.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, Buddhism spread among the common people. It became customary to visit local temples to make wishes, often by offering written prayer petitions.
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, travel became popular among the general public. While people previously visited nearby shrines and temples, they began undertaking pilgrimages to distant sites, such as {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|qid=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji|1=Zenkō-ji}} and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, to offer prayers. Various wishes were made at these temples and shrines across the country.
Before modern times, prayers involved a form of contract with the deities, such as promising to donate lanterns upon fulfillment of a wish. Unlike today’s one-sided prayers, these contracts often resulted in donations of furnishings or repairs to the facilities, many of which remain visible as of 2006.
== See Also == * {{ill|Naidōjō|ja|内道場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naidōjō|1=naidoujou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109596676}} – Private temples of Chinese emperors. * {{ill|Private chapel|ja|プライベート・チャペル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Private chapel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Chapels within European noble residences.
